questions.question: Which of the following is used to create a new HTTP server in Node.js?
questions.answer: http.createServer()
questions.explanation: http.createServer() is the method from the 'http' core module used to create an HTTP server. http.newServer() does not exist. The method requires a callback with (req, res) parameters.

questions.answer: All of the above
questions.explanation: res.end() accepts a string. JSON.stringify(obj) converts object to string, obj.toString() gives [object Object], and ''+obj also gives [object Object]. All three are valid strings and won't cause an error in res.end().

questions.question: Http module falls under which type of module?
questions.answer: Core
questions.explanation: The http module is a Core module of Node.js — it comes built-in and doesn't need to be installed via npm. Other core modules include fs, path, events, url, os.

questions.answer: It will generate an error as data must be type of string
questions.explanation: q1.query returns an object {year:'2025', month:'feb'}, not a string. fs.writeFile() requires the data parameter to be a string, Buffer, or Uint8Array. Passing an object throws a TypeError.
options: fsd2.txt file will be created and 'completed' will be displayed
fsd2.txt file will be created but 'completed' will not be displayed
fsd2.txt file will not be created but 'completed' will be displayed
It will generate an error as data must be type of string

questions.question: Which of the following NodeJS module splits up a web address into readable parts?
questions.answer: url module
questions.explanation: The url module in Node.js parses a URL string into its components: protocol, host, pathname, query, hash, etc. path module handles file paths, os module handles OS info, http module handles HTTP.

questions.answer: Hello World!
questions.explanation: Content-Type is 'text/plain', not 'text/html'. With plain text content type, the browser displays the raw string including HTML tags literally. 'Hello World!' is shown as-is, not rendered bold.
options: will print Hello World! in bold

questions.answer: /default.htm
questions.explanation: url.parse() returns an object. The pathname property contains only the path part of the URL, which is '/default.htm' (without the query string). The full URL with query is q.path.

questions.answer: ?year=2023&month=december
questions.explanation: url.parse() returns an object. The 'search' property contains the entire query string including the '?' prefix. So q1.search = '?year=2023&month=december'. The 'query' property would give the parsed object.

questions.answer: undefined
questions.explanation: url.parse(addr, false) parses the URL but does NOT parse the query string. q.query is a raw string 'name=karan&age=21', not an object. Accessing .name on a string returns undefined.

questions.question: Which method of the url module parses a URL string without parsing the query string?
questions.answer: url.parse(url, false)
questions.explanation: url.parse(url, false) or url.parse(url) keeps the query string as a raw string. url.parse(url, true) parses query string into an object. url.format() formats a URL object into a string.

questions.question: Which object provides details about the client request?
questions.answer: request
questions.explanation: The 'request' (req) object in Node.js HTTP server contains details about the incoming client request: URL, method, headers, body, etc. The 'response' (res) object is for sending back data.

questions.question: What is basic routing in Node.js?
questions.answer: Handling different URLs with conditions
questions.explanation: Basic routing in Node.js HTTP module means checking req.url and using if-else conditions to send different responses for different URL paths (like '/', '/about', '/contact').

questions.question: Which property of the request object is commonly used for basic routing in a Node.js HTTP server?
questions.answer: req.url
questions.explanation: req.url contains the URL path of the incoming request (e.g., '/', '/about'). It is used in if-else conditions for routing. req.method gives the HTTP method (GET/POST).

questions.question: Which conditional structure is commonly used to implement basic routing in a Node.js HTTP server?
questions.answer: if...else statement
questions.explanation: Basic routing checks req.url using if...else conditions to determine which response to send. For example: if(req.url === '/') { send home page } else if(req.url === '/about') { send about page }.

questions.question: Write a Node.js program to create an HTTP server using the http module that listens on port 3000 and displays different messages for / and /about routes. Explain how basic routing is implemented using the request object.
questions.explanation: req.url holds the URL path. if-else checks this path and sends different responses. This simple conditional routing is the foundation before using frameworks like Express.

questions.question: Develop a Node.js application that parses query parameters from a URL using the url module and displays them in the browser. Demonstrate the use of url.parse() with query parsing enabled.
questions.explanation: url.parse(req.url, true) parses the full request URL. The second argument 'true' enables query string parsing into an object. We loop over query properties to display them.

questions.question: Explain how request and response objects work in the HTTP module of Node.js. Write a short code snippet demonstrating the use of res.write() and res.end().
questions.answer:
REQUEST OBJECT (req): The request object represents the incoming HTTP request from the client. Key properties: - req.url: The URL path (e.g., '/about') - req.method: HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) - req.headers: Object containing request headers - req.body: Request body (for POST) RESPONSE OBJECT (res): The response object is used to send data back to the client. Key methods: - res.writeHead(statusCode, headers): Sets status code and headers - res.write(data): Sends a chunk of data (can be called multiple times) - res.end([data]): Finalizes and sends the response - res.setHeader(name, value): Sets a response header Difference between res.write() and res.end(): - res.write() sends partial response data (can be called multiple times) - res.end() signals end of response (must be called once to close connection) Code Example: const http = require('http'); http.createServer(function(req, res) { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.write(''); // Sends first chunk res.write('

Hello from Node.js!

'); // Sends second chunk res.write('

Request URL: ' + req.url + '

'); // Sends third chunk res.write(''); // Sends fourth chunk res.end(); // Finalizes the response (no more data) // OR: res.end('

Final data

'); -- sends last chunk and ends }).listen(3000);
questions.explanation: req and res are the two parameters of the createServer callback. res.write() can be called multiple times to stream data; res.end() must be called once to complete the response.

questions.question: Create a Node.js server that sends a JSON response when the user visits /api. Set the correct response headers and explain the importance of sending JSON data in web applications.
questions.explanation: Content-Type: application/json tells the client how to interpret the response. JSON.stringify() converts the JS object to a JSON string for transmission. This is the foundation of REST APIs.

questions.question: Write a node.js script to print query string of URL on console as well as on file using ES6.
questions.explanation: ES6 arrow functions are used for callbacks. url.parse with true parses the query string into an object. JSON.stringify converts it to a string for file writing and response.

questions.question: Write a node.js script to load a simple.html file on nodejs web server & prints its contents as html content.
questions.explanation: fs.readFile reads the HTML file. Setting Content-Type to 'text/html' tells the browser to render HTML. res.end(data) sends the raw file buffer — the browser renders it as HTML.

questions.question: Explain URL module with appropriate example.
questions.explanation: The url module is essential for parsing incoming request URLs in Node.js HTTP servers. url.parse() breaks the URL into components for easy access.

questions.question: What do you mean by QueryString?
questions.answer:
A QueryString is the part of a URL that contains data to be passed to web applications.

Format: URL?key1=value1&key2=value2

Example:
http://localhost:3000/search?name=John&age=25&city=Ahmedabad

Parts:
- '?' marks the start of the query string
- 'name=John' is a key-value pair
- '&' separates multiple key-value pairs

In Node.js:
const url = require('url');
const addr = 'http://localhost:3000/?name=John&age=25';
const q = url.parse(addr, true);

console.log(q.query);        // { name: 'John', age: '25' }
console.log(q.query.name);   // John
console.log(q.query.age);    // 25
console.log(q.search);       // ?name=John&age=25

Use Cases:
1. Search functionality: /search?q=nodejs
2. Filters: /products?category=phones&price=10000
3. Pagination: /posts?page=2&limit=10
4. Authentication: /login?token=abc123

Note: Values are always strings even if they look like numbers.
questions.explanation: A query string is the portion of a URL after the '?' character. It carries data from client to server as key-value pairs, widely used in GET requests for filtering and searching.

questions.question: How JSON processing is done in node js? Explain with suitable example.
questions.explanation: JSON processing in Node.js uses built-in JSON object. stringify() serializes objects for transmission or storage, parse() deserializes received JSON strings back into usable objects.

questions.question:
Write node js script to fetch values from url: 'https://www.google.com/exam.txt?c1=Hello&c2=FSD2+T1+Test&c3=Welcome+to+LJU#AllTheBest'
1) Data must be written to a file (name fetched from URL).
2) Read content from file and send response to server on '/' page in H1 red tag.
3) If any other page is requested show 'Page not found' in plain text.
questions.explanation: path.basename() extracts filename from URL path. URL query values (+ decoded as spaces) form the file content. Server reads the file and renders each line in a red H1 tag.

questions.question: Create HTTP webpage on which Home page displays 'Welcome to Log in page' in blue color and font size 32px, Login page shows one HTML file from static URL having Form with Username, Password, submit and reset button, Gallery page reflects one Image 'hello.jpg' and any other page shows 'Page Not found'.
questions.explanation: Each route is handled separately. Home uses inline HTML styles. Login reads a static HTML file. Gallery reads an image file with image/jpeg Content-Type. Other routes return 404.

questions.question: Write node.js script to print 'Welcome Admin' on home page of server. If user requests for second page it displays 'This is second page' in italic font-style and if any other request is requested it shows 'Page not found' message.
questions.explanation: Basic routing uses req.url checks. CSS inline style font-style:italic is applied to the second page text. 404 status code is set for unmatched routes.

questions.question: Write node.js script to print 'Welcome to Home Page' with two links (About Us, Contact Us) on home page. If user requests About Us page: display 'Welcome to LJ University' in bold blue. If Contact Us: display email in italic red. Any other request: 'Page not found' in plaintext.
questions.explanation: Home page uses anchor tags for navigation. About page uses <b> with blue color. Contact page uses <i> with red color. Unmatched routes return 404 plain text.

questions.question: Write a Node.js program using the HTTP module to create a server that sends a JSON response when the user visits the /api route. The JSON object should contain: name as 'Rahul', course as 'NodeJS', status as 'Enrolled'. Set the appropriate header for JSON data and display '404 – Page Not Found' for any other route.
questions.explanation: Content-Type application/json is crucial for JSON responses. JSON.stringify converts the JS object to a JSON string. Any route other than /api returns a 404 plain text response.

questions.question: Write a Node.js program that uses the URL module to parse query parameters from: http://localhost:3000/?name=yen&course=NodeJS. The server should extract the values of name and course and display them in the browser response. Explain how the url.parse() method is used to retrieve query parameters.
questions.explanation: url.parse(req.url, true) is the key. The 'true' argument parses the query string into a JavaScript object, allowing property access like query.name and query.course.

questions.question: Write a Node.js script that uses the URL module to extract values from: https://abc.com/data.txt?m1=Congrats&m2=Node+Test&m3=Best#Done. Create a file with the name from the URL and write content using URL values.
questions.explanation: path.basename() extracts 'data.txt' from the URL path. URL's + signs in query strings are decoded as spaces. hash property gives '#Done'. Content is assembled from URL components.
